name: Table Mountain
short_description: Public BLM rockhounding near Castle Valley with excellent agate, jasper, and petrified wood scattered across desert benches. No permit required, personal use only.
description:
Table Mountain sits on BLM land in Emery County, Utah, offering accessible rockhounding for collectors of all skill levels. The site is known for producing quality agates and jasper specimens found in weathered volcanic and sedimentary deposits across the rolling desert terrain. The area receives light to moderate use and provides excellent opportunities to find colorful banded agates, red and brown jasper chunks, and occasional petrified wood fragments scattered across wash beds and hillsides.

The geology here reflects the complex layering of the Mesozoic Colorado Plateau, where ancient volcanic activity and silica-rich hydrothermal solutions created the conditions for agate and chalcedony formation. Erosion over millions of years has exposed these minerals at the surface, making Table Mountain an ideal location for surface collecting without requiring excavation. The benches and washes around the mesa naturally concentrate specimens.

Expect to spend 2-4 hours exploring the accessible collecting areas. Most productive collecting occurs in dry wash beds where water runoff has concentrated lighter specimens, and along the base of small escarpments where erosion exposes fresh material. The terrain is moderately rocky but walkable, with scattered junipers and low desert shrubs providing occasional shade.

This location is ideal for families and beginners because it requires no permits, involves minimal hiking, and the specimens are relatively easy to spot once you know what to look for. The BLM allows personal use collection with a 25-pound daily limit, making it possible to fill a modest collection in a single visit.
history: Table Mountain sits within the Colorado Plateau, where Paleocene to Eocene volcanic activity deposited silica-rich minerals that crystallized into the agate and jasper found here today. Millions of years of erosion and weathering have exposed these formations at the surface, concentrating specimens in wash beds and along hillside shoulders where water and wind action continue to release new material.
tips: Search the dry wash beds first, as water runoff concentrates lighter-colored specimens and makes spotting easier. Look for banded patterns in rocks ranging from orange-red to cream colored - these are the most productive agate and jasper specimens. Bring a small magnifying glass to examine color banding in situ before picking up specimens. Use a 5-gallon bucket to organize finds and track your weight, since the 25-pound daily limit applies to all material collected.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 5,400 feet with rolling sagebrush-covered desert benches and shallow washes. Terrain is moderately rocky with scattered loose stones and small boulders. Walking distance from parking is typically 0.25 to 1.5 miles depending on which wash system you explore. Footing is uneven but manageable for most hikers; wear sturdy footwear with ankle support.
best_season: October - April. The high desert elevation and intense summer heat (often exceeding 95 degrees) make summer and early fall unpleasant for extended rockhounding. Winter temperatures are mild at this elevation, and spring offers excellent conditions with lower rainfall risk than late summer.
written_directions: From Castle Valley, drive east on Castle Valley Road toward La Sal Mountain Pass. Continue approximately 12 miles on the main road, watching for BLM boundary markers and public land signs. Look for informal pullouts and rough two-track spurs on the north side of the road near mile marker 12. Park in a pullout and hike north across open sagebrush bench toward Table Mountain's western slope. Most productive areas are within 0.5 to 1 mile of the main road.
road_conditions: Castle Valley Road is paved to the collection area, with some rough two-track spurs. High-clearance vehicles recommended for side roads, but main parking pullouts are accessible to standard passenger vehicles.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts along Castle Valley Road near the site. No developed parking lot. Capacity varies from 2-6 vehicles per pullout. Arrive early during popular seasons (spring/fall) to secure parking.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or tool-based digging. Surface collection only. Pack out all waste.
rules:
Personal use collection only
Maximum 25 pounds per day
No commercial collection or sale
No motorized equipment or powered digging tools
Surface collection only - no excavation
Pack out all trash and waste
nearest_services: Castle Valley has limited services; nearest full amenities (gas, food, water, lodging) in Moab, approximately 35 miles northwest.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most hikers with basic fitness. No wheelchair access. Parking pullouts are roadside with uneven shoulders. Main collecting areas are 0.25 to 1.5 miles from parking over open desert with no marked trail. Loose rocks and uneven ground require sturdy footwear. Shaded rest areas are limited.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find at Table Mountain near Castle Valley?
faq.answer: Table Mountain is known for producing banded red, orange, and cream-colored agates, along with solid-colored red and brown jasper specimens. Most pieces range from 1 to 6 inches in size. Petrified wood fragments also occur here, though less frequently than agate and jasper. Specimens typically require only light cleaning to reveal color.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Table Mountain in Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collection on BLM land at Table Mountain. However, you must follow BLM rules: collect only for personal use, limit yourself to 25 pounds per day, and use only surface collection methods. No commercial collection is allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Castle Valley?
faq.answer: October through April offers the best conditions. Summer heat regularly exceeds 95 degrees at this 5,400-foot elevation, making collecting uncomfortable. Spring (March-April) and fall (October-November) provide ideal temperatures and lower flash flood risk from summer monsoons.

faq.question: How far is Table Mountain from Moab, and can I reach it with a regular car?
faq.answer: Table Mountain is approximately 35 miles southeast of Moab via Castle Valley Road, roughly a 45-minute drive. The main road is paved and accessible to standard passenger vehicles. Some rough two-track spurs near the site are better suited to high-clearance vehicles, but parking pullouts are reachable by any car.

faq.question: Is Table Mountain rockhounding suitable for families with children and dogs?
faq.answer: Yes, Table Mountain is very family-friendly. The terrain is mostly level, distances from parking are short (under 1.5 miles), and no permit is required. Children ages 6 and up can easily participate. Dogs are welcome on BLM land. Pack plenty of water, sunscreen, and a hat, as shade is limited.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to collect agate and jasper at Table Mountain?
faq.answer: Surface collection only is permitted, so bring a small hand rake or brush to clear loose soil, a magnifying glass to inspect specimens, a 5-gallon bucket to carry finds and track weight, and sturdy hiking boots. A backpack with water, sunscreen, and a hat is essential for desert conditions. Avoid bringing digging tools or powered equipment.

meta_title: Table Mountain Rockhounding - Utah Agate & Jasper
meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and petrified wood at Table Mountain near Castle Valley. BLM public land, no permit required, 25 lb daily limit. Beginner-friendly.
